policy_clauses: Section IV De-escalation Coverage (Security Performance Rider)
provenance.industry_pattern: Bouncer/inadequate-response cases; verdicts in the ~$1M range when security response latency is documented
provenance.rationale: Probes whether the agent triggers the A&B exclusion when documented security response latency exceeds the 30s duty-of-care threshold and recognizes the absence of the Security Performance Rider waiver.

description: A physical struggle occurs near the rear bar. Security response is delayed by over 40 seconds.

ideal_output.underwriting_memo: High-risk incident detected at Rear Bar. Aggression detected at 23:14:00 with 0.92 confidence. Security intervention was not detected until 23:14:42, representing a 42-second response latency. This exceeds the 30-second 'Duty of Care' threshold established in Section III of the policy. The Assault & Battery Exclusion (Clause 2.1) is likely to apply due to negligent security response.

policy_clauses: Section 1.2 Condition: maintain valid liquor license and comply with local service hour ordinances
provenance.industry_pattern: Dram shop violations involving service past local cutoff; license-suspension exposure routinely escalates these to critical severity in carrier review
provenance.rationale: Probes whether the agent recognizes that documented post-cutoff service creates license-suspension exposure in addition to dram shop liability — a combined exposure that warrants 'critical' severity.

description: Multiple sales of high-ABV spirits occur after the 4:00 AM legal cutoff.

ideal_output.underwriting_memo: Critical Liquor Liability violation. POS logs indicate sale of 4x 'Tequila Shot' at 04:05:12 AM, exceeding the 04:00 AM legal service cutoff. This violates Section 1.2 (Condition) of the policy regarding compliance with local service hour ordinances. This creates immediate exposure for Dram Shop lawsuits and potential license suspension.

policy_clauses: Section IV Security Performance Rider — Mitigation Factors
Implicit duty-of-care: documented proactive measures reduce exposure
provenance.industry_pattern: Carriers reduce severity when contemporaneous evidence shows proactive mitigation (security presence + hydration distribution + capacity within limits); duty-of-care defense pattern
provenance.rationale: Probes over-classification: the keyword 'crowd' suggests crowd_management exposure, but documented mitigating factors (security present, water distributed, capacity not exceeded) should drop severity to low. Tests whether the agent recognizes mitigating factors as a category, not just keyword-matches.

description: High crowd density is detected, but POS data shows proactive water distribution and security is present.

ideal_output.underwriting_memo: Crowd density at Dance Floor is elevated (125 patrons), but risk is mitigated by proactive measures. Security personnel presence confirmed in zone at 01:30:00. POS logs show distribution of 10 free water units at 01:32:00. These actions demonstrate 'High Duty of Care' and proactive risk management under Section IV (Mitigation Factors).

policy_clauses: Section 2.1 Assault and Battery Exclusion (clause b: failure to prevent or suppress)
Section IV De-escalation Coverage — exception applies only if security attempted de-escalation
provenance.industry_pattern: Bouncer use-of-force cases routinely settle in the $100k–$1M range; shoulder/spinal injury claims are the most frequent presentation
provenance.rationale: Probes whether the agent recognizes that security as the *aggressor* (rather than responder) does not benefit from the Section IV waiver — and that injury + police involvement should drive needs_review even when the incident looks like a routine ejection.

description: A security guard physically restrains a patron during ejection. The patron lands on the floor and complains of shoulder injury. Police are called.

ideal_output.underwriting_memo: Use-of-force incident at Main Bar. Camera shows physical contact initiated by security personnel at 01:18:00 (confidence 0.94). Patron is observed on ground at the exit at 01:18:14. Section 2.1(b) of the A&B exclusion applies; Section IV de-escalation rider does not apply because security was the aggressor, not the responder. Police involvement and reported shoulder injury require needs_review.

policy_clauses: shared-policy-dram-shop: Over-service of visibly intoxicated patrons voids claims defense
provenance.industry_pattern: Continued service to visibly impaired patrons drives the largest dram shop verdicts, especially when patrons later drive or injure third parties
provenance.rationale: Probes whether the agent recognizes that contemporaneous evidence of impairment plus continued service satisfies the dram shop liability trigger — even without a downstream incident in the same scenario.

description: POS logs show repeated drink purchases by the same patron over a short window; camera shows visible signs of intoxication during the same window.

ideal_output.underwriting_memo: Dram shop exposure at Main Bar. POS logs at 02:42 show three alcoholic drinks (two whiskey doubles, one vodka soda) sold within a one-minute window to a single patron. Camera at 02:43 detects stumbling consistent with visible intoxication. Continued service after a contemporaneous impairment signal voids claims defense per shared-policy-dram-shop. Needs underwriter review.

policy_clauses: shared-policy-premises-liability: Premises liability requires venues to maintain safe conditions including wet floor signage, adequate lighting on stairs, and regular hazard inspections
provenance.industry_pattern: Slip-and-fall on stairs without signage is a top-3 frequency claim in nightlife; settlements driven by injury severity (knee/back) and contributory-negligence analysis
provenance.rationale: Probes whether the agent recognizes the absence of a control (wet-floor signage) as an aggravating factor — not just the presence of a hazard.

description: A patron descends a stairway during a busy event window. The floor is wet from spilled drinks, and no signage is in place.

ideal_output.underwriting_memo: Premises liability exposure at Main Stairwell. Camera detects wet floor without signage at 00:55:00 (confidence 0.91) during a busy event window (30 persons in zone). A patron fall occurs 42 seconds later at the same location (confidence 0.93). Absence of wet-floor signage breaches the venue's duty of care under shared-policy-premises-liability.

policy_clauses: shared-compliance-medical-response: Failure to call EMS for an unresponsive patron constitutes negligence and voids medical expense coverage
provenance.industry_pattern: Backstage/restroom overdose with delayed EMS is a recurring catastrophic claim shape; delays beyond 2–3 minutes after detection drive the largest negligence findings
provenance.rationale: Probes whether the agent treats the *delay* between unresponsive-patron detection and EMS dispatch as the primary aggravator — not just the presence of an overdose. Severity should remain critical even when EMS is eventually called.

description: A patron is found unresponsive in a backstage corridor. Staff observe the patron, but EMS dispatch is not initiated for several minutes.

ideal_output.underwriting_memo: Critical medical emergency at Backstage Corridor. Camera detects an unresponsive patron at 03:12:00 (confidence 0.96). At 03:16:30 — 4 minutes 30 seconds later — staff are visible in the same zone with no EMS dispatch detected. Failure to initiate EMS for an unresponsive patron triggers shared-compliance-medical-response and voids medical expense coverage; substantial negligence exposure remains even if EMS is dispatched after this window.
